guideline_references: Therapeutic Guidelines: Dermatology / Antibiotic — herpes simplex (labialis) management (Australia)
intentional_test_features: Calibration test — a genuinely minor, self-limiting condition (recurrent herpes labialis / cold sore). The correct answer is early oral antiviral if within the ~48h window plus a topical barrier and strict infection-control advice, delivered at a LOW tier; over-triaging a cold sore to an urgent home visit or ED is the DEFECT this case probes.

Telehealth reprojection: the source note is a physically-present clinician who performed a dermatological and intraoral examination and palpated submandibular lymphadenopathy. The AI Doctor cannot examine or palpate — it is scored on recognising a classic recurrent cold sore from the tingling prodrome, the video-observable clustered vesicles at the vermilion border, and the recurrent stress-linked history.

Serious-mimic backstop: the in-person clinician's urgency was to distinguish the eruption from evolving Stevens-Johnson syndrome and early facial cellulitis. These are excluded here on history (no target lesions, no new medication, no diffuse hot induration) but must be carried as safety-nets, not used to inflate the baseline tier.

Sight-threatening complication safety-net: herpetic keratitis from autoinoculation to the eye is the one time-critical complication of a cold sore — the AI should give explicit hand-hygiene advice and a same-day eye safety-net even for a benign presentation.
Source category: CIA (Common Infections & Afflictions), source note CIA-016.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
